coredata.dc:title: Stimulus-responsive room-temperature phosphorescence with tunable color for time-resolved advanced dynamic anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Li
Zhang, Qi
Ma, Yatuan
Li, Hongjuan
Sun, Shiguo
Xu, Yongqian
coredata.dc:description: Multi-stimuli responsive multicolored room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have recently attracted considerable attention, owing to their potential applications in advanced dynamic anti-counterfeiting. The design and development of carbon dots that exhibit both stimulus response characteristics and multicolor RTP emission remains a significant challenge in the field. In this work, we present a novel approach to control the dynamic phosphorescence color and stimulus response properties of CQDs coated on paper by fine-tuning the surface functional groups of the CQDs. Specifically, histidine and 2,4,6-triaminopyrimidine was separately used to prepare N, O co-doped CQDs (NOCQDs) and N-doped CQDs (NCQDs), respectively. These CQDs were further coated on filter paper substrates to achieve water and heating cascade stimulus–response RTP. These stimuli-responsive properties of NOCQDs can be modulated by deprotonating their surface oxygen-containing groups. Eventually, we achieved time-resolved multicolor dynamic anti-counterfeiting by exploiting the stimuli-response properties and lifetimes differences between NOCQDs and NCQDs, realizing the 5D information encryption in all five stimulus-, pattern-, time-, and color-dependent codes. These findings highlight the potential of NOCQDs as advanced anti-counterfeiting materials and lay a foundation for further exploration into the synthesis and properties of CQDs with tunable stimuli-response features and RTP emission.

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the NOCQDs and NCQDs inks
Evaluation of cytotoxicity of NQCQDs and NCQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic process of NOCQDs and schematic diagram of intermolecular interaction changes and the generation of RTP with different colors as NOCQDs ethanol solution was coated on paper and underwent heating to dry, irradiation with different excitation wavelengths.
TEM image and HRTEM image of NCQDs (a) and NOCQDs (c), respectively. Size distribution of NCQDs (b) and NOCQDs (d), respectively.

(a) XRD patterns of NCQDs and NOCQDs. (b) FTIR spectra of NCQDs and NOCQDs. (c) XPS survey spectrum of NCQDs. NCQDs high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (d), N1s (e). (f) XPS spectra of NOCQDs. NOCQDs high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (g), N1s (h) and O1s (i).

The phosphorescence spectra of NCQDs (1), paper (2), and the NCQDs ethanol solution-coated paper after being treated with heating (3), water (4), cascade heating and water (5) and cascade water and heating (6), Ex = 254 nm (a) and 365 nm (b). Phosphorescence lifetime of the NCQDs under excitation at 254 nm (c) and 365 nm (d). (e) Corresponding photographs of the NCQDs coated on paper after being treated with cascade water and heating under excitation of 254 or 365 nm light and the cease of excitation.

The phosphorescence spectra of various samples upon excitation at 254 nm (a) and 365 nm (b), respectively. (1): NOCQDs; (2): paper; (3) NOCQDs coated-paper after being treated with heating; (4): NOCQDs coated-paper after being treated with water; (5): NOCQDs coated-paper after being treated with cascade heating and water; (6): NOCQDs coated-paper after being treated with cascade water and heating. Phosphorescence lifetime of NOCQDs under excitation of 254 nm (c) and 365 nm (d). (e) Corresponding photographs of NOCQDs coated on paper after being treated with cascade water and heating under excitation of 254 or 365 nm UV lamp and the cease of excitation.
Schematic diagram of intermolecular interaction changes as NOCQDs or deprotonated NOCQDs ethanol solution was coated on paper and underwent heating to dry.

The decryption process of anti-counterfeiting information (lotus) that was printed with the NOCQDs ethanol solution. The decryption process includes different treatment modes, e.g. water treatment, heating treatment, cascade water and heating treatment, exposure to air after heating treatment, irradiation with 254 or 356 nm light and then the cessation of irradiation.

The decryption process of anti-counterfeiting information (lotus) that was printed with the NOCQDs ethanol solution and NOCQDs aqueous solution. The decryption process includes different treatment modes, e.g. water treatment, heating treatment, cascade water and heating treatment, exposure to air after heating treatment, irradiation with 254 or 356 nm light and then the cessation of irradiation.

Schematic illustration of the time-resolved advanced dynamic anti-counterfeiting based on the NOCQDs and NCQDs. The decryption of anti-counterfeiting information (flowers) that was printed with the NOCQDs and NCQDs ethanol solution is performed through water and heating treatment.

The construction of time-dependent dynamic RTP tags, the writing and reading of anti-counterfeiting information, and digital photographs of binary coded RTP data storage chips. Three colors are designated as three decimal numbers for encryption, with blue = 1, cyan = 2, green = 3, and the default dark (filter paper) = 0. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Stimulus-responsive room-temperature phosphorescence with tunable color for time-resolved advanced dynamic anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Natural Products & Chemical Biology, College of Chemistry & Pharmacy, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Natural Products & Chemical Biology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Natural Products & Chemical Biology, College of Chemistry & Pharmacy, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Materials and Safety Technology, College of Petroleum and Chemical Engineering, Beibu Gulf University, Qinzhou, Guangxi, 515000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Materials and Safety Technology
College of Petroleum and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Materials and Safety Technology, College of Petroleum and Chemical Engineering, Beibu Gulf University, Qinzhou, Guangxi, 515000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Time-resolved multicolor dynamic anti-counterfeiting was achieved by exploiting the stimuli-response properties and lifetimes differences between NOCQDs and NCQDs, realizing the 5D information encryption in all five stimulus-, pattern-, time-, and color-dependent codes.

Multi-stimuli responsive multicolored room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have recently attracted considerable attention, owing to their potential applications in advanced dynamic anti-counterfeiting. The design and development of carbon dots that exhibit both stimulus response characteristics and multicolor RTP emission remains a significant challenge in the field. In this work, we present a novel approach to control the dynamic phosphorescence color and stimulus response properties of CQDs coated on paper by fine-tuning the surface functional groups of the CQDs. Specifically, histidine and 2,4,6-triaminopyrimidine was separately used to prepare N, O co-doped CQDs (NOCQDs) and N-doped CQDs (NCQDs), respectively. These CQDs were further coated on filter paper substrates to achieve water and heating cascade stimulus–response RTP. These stimuli-responsive properties of NOCQDs can be modulated by deprotonating their surface oxygen-containing groups. Eventually, we achieved time-resolved multicolor dynamic anti-counterfeiting by exploiting the stimuli-response properties and lifetimes differences between NOCQDs and NCQDs, realizing the 5D information encryption in all five stimulus-, pattern-, time-, and color-dependent codes. These findings highlight the potential of NOCQDs as advanced anti-counterfeiting materials and lay a foundation for further exploration into the synthesis and properties of CQDs with tunable stimuli-response features and RTP emission.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The global issue of counterfeiting poses severe ramifications on the stability of the economy and the rights of consumers [1]. Consequently, the utilization of stimulus-responsive luminescent materials has been an efficacious solution to combat the counterfeit challenge. Such materials are also referred to as “smart” materials, as their luminescent properties (including wavelength, intensity, and lifetime) can be modified by diverse external stimuli, such as heating, light, magnetic fields, pH, and solvents [2,3]. By incorporating external stimuli and emissive colors, intensity, and lifetimes, it becomes feasible to construct a multimodal anti-counterfeiting information monitoring window, enabling the identification of product authenticity and corresponding information, thus effectively thwarting counterfeit activities [4]. In the realm of anti-counterfeiting, luminescent materials that are responsive to multiple external stimuli have garnered greater interest than those that respond to a single stimulus [5–7]. This is particularly true for materials that display specific logical relationships between various external stimuli. Such materials possess more intricate decryption programs, rendering it impossible to decode anti-counterfeiting information using incorrect decryption sequences [8–10]. Similar to password locks, specific external stimuli and sequential inputs are required to confirm the authenticity of anti-counterfeiting information accurately [11,12]. Consequently, luminescent materials with specific logical orders offer and multi-stimuli-responsiveness heightened accuracy and security for anti-counterfeiting measures.

Amid the vast array of stimulus-responsive light-emitting materials, molecular persistent light-emitting materials, e.g., room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials, have emerged as a prominent research area in time-dependent dynamic anti-counterfeiting, due to their distinct singlet–triplet state light-emitting mechanism, large Stokes shift, long lifetime, and zero background interference properties [13–15]. These materials offer significant advantages over static and prompt fluorescent materials in the areas of multicolor anti-counterfeiting and information encryption [16,17]. Recent advances in the field of RTP materials have focused on the precise adjustment of the composition and structure of RTP emission centers, along with specific combinations of various single-emission RTP materials, resulting in significant progress in the development of time-dependent phosphorescence color (TDPC) for realizing high-security information encryption. However, these RTP materials still suffer from several limitations in their practical applications. For instance, they may only respond to a single external stimulus and rarely to multiple external stimuli [18]. Additionally, most RTP materials emit in crystal or solid state, resulting in poor processability and low safety [19]. Doping RTP materials with precious metals and metal elements can also lead to high preparation costs and toxicity risks [20]. Furthermore, the single-emission color exhibited by most stimulus-responsive RTP materials may not perform well in practical applications that require multidimensional signals and multiple colors to improve safety [21]. Thus, the achievement of high-level anti-counterfeiting measures necessitates the investigation of non-metallic multi-color RTP materials exhibiting time-resolved properties capable of responding to diverse external stimuli.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a type of zero-dimensional functional material that has been extensively studied. They possess unique properties such as tunable emission bands, good biocompatibility, and a simple preparation process [22]. The introduction of heteroatoms (such as O, N, S, P) into CQDs can create different functional groups on their surfaces [23]. These functional groups contribute to the rich energy levels and strong affinity for external molecules or ions, which enables stimulus-responsive multicolor fluorescence emission [24]. To develop polychromatic RTP materials, researchers have successfully embedded luminescent CQDs into various matrices such as nano silica, metal–organic framework materials (MOFs), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), urea, boron oxide, etc [25]. This strategy involves creating a rigid structure within the matrix that stabilizes the CQDs and their triplets. By carefully controlling the embedding matrix, researchers can achieve RTP characteristics in CQDs. However, the matrix can also block the surface functional groups on CQDs, limiting the generation of stimulus-responsive RTP, let alone multi-stimulus-responsive RTP. Moreover, the shielding of surface active groups can affect the final RTP color. While CQDs are an ideal choice for constructing stimulus-responsive multicolor RTP materials, simultaneously controlling multicolor luminescent centers and stimulus-responsive sites is a complex task that has yet to be fully realized [26]. Additionally, the specific correlation and regulatory mechanism between the surface functional groups of CQDs and their stimulus–response luminescence characteristics in RTP systems remain unclear. This lack of understanding poses challenges in the practical application of CQDs in anti-counterfeiting technology. By integrating these features into CQDs, their potential application range, especially in advanced dynamic anti-counterfeiting fields, can be significantly expanded.

In this study, we developed and synthesized two types of CQDs: NOCQDs and NCQDs. These CQDs have different surface functional groups. The NOCQDs contain surface groups such as –NH2, –COOH, and –OH, while the NCQDs only have numerous amino groups (Fig. 1 ). These functional groups act as active sites for stimulus response. By depositing these CQDs on paper and treating them with water and heat, we achieved continuous stimulus-responsive RTP, resulting in multi-color luminescence with long lifetimes. We observed multi-color RTP with long lifetime when irradiating the as-synthesized CQDs with different excitation wavelengths. To understand the relationship between the surface functional groups and their role in stimulus response and multi-color luminescence, we investigated the effect of deprotonation of these groups on luminescence. The luminescent patterns obtained from the combination of the two types of CQDs exhibit order-dependent multi-stimuli-responsive and multi-color RTP characteristics. The information hidden in the patterns can only be deciphered correctly if all of the following requirements are matched: (1) using the right combination of stimuli (water and heating), (2) the correct order of applying the stimuli, (3) irradiating the patterns with appropriate excitation wavelengths, (4) reading the information with proper delay of RTP. Due to the unique stimuli-responsive properties and distinct lifetime differences between NOCQDs and NCQDs, the findings enable multicolor time-resolved anti-counterfeiting. The encrypted information becomes practically impossible to decrypt, ensuring unparalleled information security. The discovery presents a novel approach to regulate the multi-color luminescence center and stimulus response active sites of CQDs, facilitating their application in multi-level anti-counterfeiting.

NOCQDs and NCQDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method, and their solutions exhibited good stability without obvious precipitation after long-term storage. The morphology of NOCQDs and NCQDs was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). As observed in Fig. 2 a–d, NOCQDs and NCQDs are nanoparticle spheres with good dispersion, and the average particle size is approximately 2.2 nm. HRTEM images reveal that NCQDs and NOCQDs are highly crystalline, and the crystal plane spacing is clearly visible at 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon, thereby indicating the successful synthesis of carbon dots [27]. Additionally, Fig. 3 a depicts the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of both NOCQDs and NCQDs, revealing a distinct diffraction peak at 2θ = 24.3°, which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite carbon. This result confirms that the prepared CQDs possess a graphite-like structure [27].

The surface functional groups and chemical element compositions of NOCQDs and NCQDs were analyzed to provide insights into their physicochemical properties and potential applications. The surface functional groups of NOCQDs and NCQDs were further investigated using FTIR analysis. As depicted in Fig. 3b, NCQDs exhibit three distinct absorption peaks at 3427, 1567, and 1413 cm−1, corresponding to the –NH2, CN, and C-N bonds/groups, respectively [28]. These results indicate that the primary functional group on the surface of NCQDs is –NH2. In contrast, NOCQDs display a broad absorption band at 3131 cm−1 which can be attributed to –OH and –NH2 groups. The absorption peaks at 1580, 1394, and 1350 cm−1 correspond to the CO, C-O-C, and C-N bonds/groups, respectively, indicating the presence of various functional groups such as –OH, CO, and –NH2 on the surface of NOCQDs [29].

The surface chemical element composition of NOCQDs and NCQDs was analyzed through XPS analysis. As demonstrated in Fig. 3c, NCQDs contain C and N elements in percentages of 88.68 % and 11.32 %, respectively. The high-resolution XPS C1s spectrum of NCQDs at binding energies of 284.6, 285.1, 286.7, and 288.3 eV corresponds to C-C/CC, C–NH, CC–N, and CN functional groups (Fig. 3d), respectively [29]. Additionally, the high-resolution N1s (Fig. 3e) plot of NCQDs indicated the presence of Primic N (399.6 eV), Pyrolic N (400.9 eV), and Graphical N (400.1 eV) [30]. On the other hand, NOCQDs predominantly displayed C, N, and O elements, with C accounting for 69.62 %, N accounting for 18.52 %, and O accounting for 11.85 % (Fig. 3f). The C1s high-resolution XPS spectra of NOCQDs confirmed the existence of C-C/CC (284.5 eV), C-N (285.2 eV), C-OH/C-O-C (286.1 eV), and CO (288.1 eV) bonds/functional groups (Fig. 3g). The N1s high-resolution XPS spectra of NOCQDs at binding energies of 398.6, 400.1, and 401.1 eV corresponded to Primic N, Pyrrolic N, and Graphical N, respectively (Fig. 3h). The O1s high-resolution XPS spectra of NOCQDs revealed the presence of C-OH/C-O-C (531.4 eV) and CO (533.1 eV) (Fig. 3i) [31]. The results from XPS analysis further support the findings obtained from FTIR spectroscopic analysis of NOCQDs and NCQDs. These findings demonstrate significant differences in surface functional groups between NOCQDs and NCQDs, with NCQDs mainly containing –NH2, while NOCQDs contain –NH2, –OH, and –COOH functional groups.

To investigate the optical properties of NOCQDs and NCQDs, fluorescence emission spectra were recorded for their aqueous solutions under different excitation wavelengths. As illustrated in Fig. S1a, the fluorescence emission wavelength of NCQDs shifts from 360 nm to 380 nm as the excitation wavelength increases from 255 nm to 355 nm, exhibiting faint excitation-dependent fluorescence [32]. In contrast, NCOQDs shows an evident excitation-dependent fluorescence. As shown in Fig. S1b, the fluorescence emission wavelength of NCOQDs displays a significant red shift from 352 nm to 467 nm as the excitation wavelength increases from 250 nm to 400 nm, indicating richer energy level transitions. It is worth noting that the distribution of particles with different sizes and surface states in carbon dots can lead to excitation-dependent fluorescence generation [33]. The relatively insignificant red shift in the fluorescence emission wavelength of NCQDs at different excitation wavelengths suggests that they have a relatively pure surface state with a more uniform size distribution. The particle size distribution of NCOQDs and NCQDs is consistent, indicating that their significant excitation-dependent luminescence properties are mainly related to their surface states. Specifically, the presence of a large number of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of NOCQDs can create many surface defects, leading to a slight redshift in their emission wavelength [34,35]. On the other hand, the various functional groups (–NH2, –COOH, and –OH) present on the surface of NCOQDs can generate multiple energy levels and corresponding electron transitions between energy gaps, inducing excitation-dependent fluorescence generation [36,37].

After measuring the fluorescence emission spectra, the ethanol solutions of NOCQDs and NCQDs were deposited onto filter paper, and then dried and analyzed for their RTP properties. Interestingly, no phosphorescence emission was observed from NOCQDs and NCQDs coated on paper after heating to drying. However, their phosphorescence response was elicited by continuous water and heating. The continuous-stimulus response RTP of NOCQDs and NCQDs coated on paper was further investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. 4 a–e and 5a–e, individual heating or water treatment of NOCQDs and NCQDs coated on paper did not result in phosphorescence emission, nor did first heating followed by water treatment. Their phosphorescence response was observed only upon treatment with continuously added water followed by heating, demonstrating order-dependent stimuli-responsive RTP. NOCQDs coated on paper exhibited significant excitation-dependent phosphorescence in response to continuous water and heating treatment, with RTP emission wavelength varying from approximately 480 to 541 nm as the excitation wavelength changed from 254 to 420 nm, revealing the existence of multiple triplet state emission centers (Fig. S1d). Conversely, NCQDs coated on paper subjected to continuous water and heating treatment did not demonstrate excitation-dependent phosphorescence (Fig. S1c). There was no noticeable change in RTP emission wavelength of NCQDs coated on paper upon alteration of excitation wavelength from 254 to 420 nm, suggesting the presence of a single triplet state emission center. Under excitation at 254 and 365 nm, NCQDs coated on paper demonstrated bright blue phosphorescence and an afterglow of nearly 2 s was visible (Fig. 4c–e), with phosphorescence emission wavelengths at 449 and 455 nm, and phosphorescence lifetimes of 67.32 and 240.53 ms, accordingly. However, under excitation at 254 and 365 nm, NOCQDs coated on paper revealed bright cyan and green phosphorescence and an afterglow of nearly 6 s was visible (Fig. 5 a–e), with phosphorescence emission wavelengths at 480 and 514 nm, and phosphorescence lifetimes of 801.92 and 529.85 ms, correspondingly. Thus, the striking lifetime and RTP color differences of NOCQDs and NCQDs coated on paper under the same excitation wavelength could be exploited for time-resolved dynamic anti-counterfeiting purposes.

In order to investigate the source of fluorescence and phosphorescence in NCQDs and NOCQDs, the absorption spectra were measured. As depicted in Fig. S1e, the absorption spectra of NCQDs display wider absorption peaks located around 219 and 320 nm, respectively, which correspond to the π-π* transition of CC and the n-π* transition of CN bonds [38]. The fluorescence excitation wavelength of NCQDs and the phosphorescence excitation wavelength of NCQDs embedded in paper coincide with 290 nm, which substantially overlaps with the absorption peak at 320 nm in the UV spectrum of NCQDs. Consequently, the π-π* transition of CC and CN bond-related groups may serve as the origin of fluorescence and phosphorescence in NCQDs. Additionally, the absorption spectrum of NOCQDs exhibits a strong wide absorption peak ranging from 220 to 400 nm, attributed to the π-π* transition of CC, the n-π* transition of CN, and the n-π* transition of CO, respectively (Fig. S1f) [39]. The excitation wavelength of NOCQDs embedded in paper and the fluorescence excitation wavelength of NCQDs remarkably coincide with the broad absorption peak of NOCQDs in the UV spectrum spanning from 220 to 400 nm. In this context, the π-π* transitions of CC, CN bonds, and the n-π* transitions of CO may contribute to the generation of fluorescence and phosphorescence in NOCQDs [40]. The fluorescence lifetimes (Fig. S2a) of NCQDs and NOCQDs are 3.78 ns and 5.79 ns, respectively. The fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra of NOCQDs coated paper indicate that there is a small shift between fluorescence and phosphorescence. The small energy gap between S1 and T1 denotes that electrons can produce effective ISC. Because of excitation-dependency of both fluorescence and phosphorescence, the ΔE st gradually increases from 0.31 to 0.50 eV with the excitation wavelength increasing from 255 to 395 nm (Fig. S1a–d). Moreover, from the low temperature fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of NOCQDs coated paper (Fig. S2b–d), the ΔE st is calculated to be 0.12 (254 nm excitation) and 0.43 eV (365 nm excitation). The change of ΔE st at room temperature may be attributed to the activated vibrational relaxation at elevated temperature, which enhances the ISC efficiency (Fig. S5e–f and Fig. S6e–f). In addition, the ΔE st of NCQDs coated paper slightly changes from 0.50 to 0.55 eV with the excitation wavelength increasing from 255 to 395 nm (Fig. S1a–d), being attributed to the excitation-independency of both fluorescence and phosphorescence of NCQDs coated paper.

Constructing high-density hydrogen bonding networks between CQDs and substrates has indeed been shown to be an effective strategy for obtaining RTP [41]. The use of carbonization to produce NOCQDs and NCQDs allows the heteroatomic groups (–NH2, –OH, and –COOH) incorporated in these materials to engage in hydrogen bond networks with hydroxyl groups present in the paper. These hydrogen-bonded networks serve to effectively stabilize the triplet state of NOCQDs and NCQDs, while also increasing their rigidity, hindering their movement and rotation, and minimizing non-radiative transitions, thus leading to the occurrence of RTPs [42]. To validate the creation of hydrogen bonds between two CQDs and paper, we conducted differential thermal scanning (DSC) experiments on NOCQDs and NCQDs coated on paper after continuous water and heating treatment. As presented in Fig. S3, the DSC curves of NCQDs coated on paper and NOCQDs coated on paper reveal two evident endothermic transitions, approximately located at 10–40 °C and 80 °C, respectively. These temperature intervals correspond to discontinuities in the hydrogen bond networks formed among NOCQDs, or among NCQDs, as well as the decomposition of hydrogen bonds between paper and NOCQDs or NCQDs. Thus, the DSC results confirm the establishment of hydrogen bonds between NOCQDs and NCQDs aqueous solutions embedded in paper after drying [43,44].

Furthermore, when embedded in paper, NOCQDs and NCQDs only exhibit phosphorescence following heating and drying, while unfixed NOCQDs and NCQDs powders do not produce phosphorescence, providing further support for the significance of the hydrogen bonding network formed between CQDs and paper in generating RTP. To provide additional evidence to support this hypothesis, we performed phosphorescence spectra of NOCQDs coated on paper at low temperature without water and heating treatment. The results revealed that the phosphorescence wavelength of NOCQDs coated on paper without water and heating treatment under 254 and 365 nm excitation was comparable to that of NOCQDs coated on paper treated with water and heating at room temperature (Fig. S2f). These findings further support the proposed hypothesis [45].

The abundance of –NH2, –OH, and –COOH groups on the surface of NOCQDs and NCQDs can act as donors or acceptors for hydrogen bonding [42]. Therefore, hydrogen bonding networks can be established among NOCQDs, as well as NCQDs. However, when an ethanol solution of NOCQDs and NCQDs is coated onto paper, there is a lack of an effective hydrogen bonding network between the two synthesized CQDs and cellulose chains in paper. As we know, water is a potent hydrogen bond disrupting agent that can break the hydrogen bonds present among NCQDs, NOCQDs, and cellulose in paper. This results in the formation of hydrogen bonding networks between the two synthesized CQDs and cellulose. These hydrogen bonding networks can effectively solidify the triplet state of the as-synthesized CQDs, increase rigidity, inhibit non-radiative decay, and promote the production of RTP.

To further emphasize the significance of hydrogen bonding between NOCQDs and paper in generating RTP, an alkali treatment was carried out on NOCQDs to alter the state of oxygen-containing groups (–OH and –COOH) on their surface. As shown in Figs. 6 and Fig. S4b, when the ethanol solution of NOCQDs treated with alkali was coated onto the paper and heated to dryness, bright phosphorescence emission was observed, and its phosphorescence emission wavelength aligned with that of the paper coated with the ethanol solution of NOCQDs without alkali treatment after continuous water treatment and heating. This is due to the fact that –OH and –COOH present on the NOCQDs surface produce negative oxygen through deprotonation in alkaline conditions, the repulsive effect between oxygen negatives leading to the inability to establish hydrogen bonding networks between NOCQDs and NOCQDs. However, negative oxygen can participate in the creation of multiple hydrogen bonding networks with the hydroxyl groups on the paper, thereby allowing the alkali-treated NOCQDs to directly form hydrogen bonds with the cellulose in the paper to stabilize the triplet state of NOCQDs and produce RTP [46]. Since –NH2 is not significantly influenced under alkaline conditions, the ethanol solution of NCQDs treated with alkali was coated onto the paper and dried, but no phosphorescence emission was detected (Fig. 6 and Fig. S4a). Therefore, the –OH and –COOH on the NOCQDs surface serve as active sites for water stimulus response, and the switchable hydrogen bonding network is crucial for NOCQDs and NCQDs in obtaining stimulus response RTP.

The fact that the triplet state is easily quenched by oxygen, prompts us to investigate whether heating can remove oxygen from the paper. To investigate the potential for oxygen removal via heating, a comparative analysis of RTP spectra obtained from NOCQDs coated on paper and NCQDs coated on paper subjected to vacuum conditions and those heated in ambient air is warranted. This approach aims to elucidate the impact of heating on oxygen removal from the paper and its subsequent influence on RTP. As illustrated in Fig. S5a, b, and Fig. S6a, b, the RTP spectra of NOCQDs-P and NCQDs-P exhibited good consistency after vacuum degassing at room temperature and thermal activation in air, indicating that heating eliminates oxygen from the paper and develops an oxygen barrier on the surface of the paper, weakening the quenching impact of oxygen on NOCQDs and NCQDs coated on paper phosphorescence. As demonstrated in Fig. S5c and Fig. S6c, the RTP of NCQDs coated on paper gradually opens as the heating temperature increases, reaching its maximum value at 120 °C. When the heating temperature surpasses 120 °C, the RTP of NCQDs coated on paper is rapidly quenched, following non-radiative decay induced by high temperature. As illustrated in Fig. S5d and Fig. S6d, the RTP of NOCQDs and NCQDs coated on paper undergoes air quenching after heating and cooling in air, possessing excellent reversibility and sustaining good stability even after multiple cycles.

Given the need for excellent selectivity and biocompatibility in practical anti-counterfeiting applications, the RTP selectivity of NOCQDs and NCQDs coated on paper for water was initially assessed. As indicated in Fig. S7, after the removal of 254 and 365 nm light irradiation, the RTP of NOCQDs and NCQDs coated on paper can solely be activated by water. Typical organic solvents, metal ions, anions, and physiological molecules do not significantly impact their RTP, implicating good selectivity for water.

To mitigate the potential influence of NaOH and HCl, we introduced HCl and NaOH into neutral water to generate water samples with varying pH levels to isolate and eliminate the impact of NaOH and HCl on the RTP of NOCQDs and NCQDs coated on paper. As shown in Fig. S7, both NOCQDs and NCQDs coated on paper can trigger RTP after being treated with water under pH values ranging from 2 to 10 and heated, indicating that the RTP of NOCQDs and NCQDs coated on paper can effectively isolate and eliminate the impact of NaOH and HCl. To assess the biocompatibility of NOCQDs and NCQDs, the MTT method was employed to evaluate their cytotoxicity. As demonstrated in Fig. S8, after incubating HL7702 cells with various concentrations of NOCQDs and NCQDs solutions for 24 h, even at high concentrations of 300 µg/mL of NOCQDs and NCQDs, the cell survival rate still exceeded 90 %. These results indicate that NOCQDs and NCQDs possess excellent biocompatibility, ensuring that their practical use will not pose any risk to the user's physical health.

Due to NOCQDs and NCQDs coated on paper exceptional water and heating continuous stimulation response, multi-color RTP emission, lifetimes characteristics, and good stability and biocompatibility, we propose an application of NOCQDs and NCQDs as anti-counterfeiting security inks in advanced dynamic anti-counterfeiting. As depicted in Fig. 7 , lotus patterns printed on paper using ethanol solutions of NOCQDs under the daylight existed are colorless, while showing the bright-blue fluorescence with 254 nm and 365 light irradiation. Hence, under the daylight and UV light, it is hard to distinguish the authenticity of anti-counterfeiting information. Particularly, after removing 254 and 365 nm light, the bright blue/green phosphorescent lotus patterns printed on paper using NOCQDs ethanol solution can only be observed after cascade water and heating processing sequence. It cannot be read if treated with water or heating treatment only, or an incorrect processing sequence. Interestingly, due to the quenching effect of oxygen in the air on RTP, the RTP of anti-counterfeiting patterns printed on NOCQDs rapidly quenches after continuous water and heating, and different excitation wavelengths of light stimulation. This feature is highly secure and difficult to imitate by counterfeiters.

Due to the RTP of NOCQDs aqueous and ethanol solutions coated on paper can be turned on by heating and quenched by air under environmental conditions, their applicability as safety inks for advanced dynamic anti-counterfeiting was further studied. Fig. 8 show that the “branches and leaves” and “flower” areas of lotus flowers sprayed with NOCQDs aqueous solution and NOCQDs ethanol solution, respectively, did not emit RTP after removing ultraviolet light. Interestingly, due to the excellent dispersibility of NOCQDs in water and ethanol, as well as the consistency of fluorescence emission, the “branches, leaves” and “flower” regions of lotus flowers printed with NOCQDs aqueous solution and ethanol dispersion cannot be correctly resolved under both UV light on and off conditions. After heating, significant RTP can be observed in the branches and leaves of lotus flowers sprayed with NOCQDs aqueous solution after turning off ultraviolet light irradiation. Due to the quenching effect of water on RTP, the RTP in the spray printing area of NOCQDs aqueous solution rapidly quenches after adding water. After heating again to remove free water and oxygen from the paper, the RTP in the area sprayed with NOCQDs aqueous solution and ethanol dispersion can be simultaneously activated, thus the complete RTP lotus pattern can be observed. In addition, NOCQDs can be used for high-level anti-counterfeiting of paper and paper money (Fig. S9). The multi-color RTP of NOCQDs in paper money can only be achieved by continuous water and heating treatment, demonstrating reliable applicability and practical value.

NOCQDs exhibits a unique combination of multiple stimulus responses and multi-color RTP, which sets it apart from similar materials reported in the literature (Table S1). This distinctive feature makes it highly suitable for anti-counterfeiting applications that require enhanced security measures. The material's ability to respond to cascade water and heating treatment, further strengthens its anti-counterfeiting capabilities. Additionally, its multi-color luminescence property adds an extra layer of security, as it enables easy identification and authentication.

Taking into consideration the significant difference in phosphorescence lifetime and stimulus response characteristics between NOCQDs coated on paper and NCQDs coated on paper, their potential application in resolving dynamic anti-counterfeiting during stimulus response was further evaluated. NOCQDs and NCQDs were utilized to print different regions of anti-counterfeiting information. As depicted in Fig. 9 , after continuous water and heating treatment, the “branches and leaves” of the flowers in the anti-counterfeiting pattern printed with NCQDs exhibit bright blue RTP after 254 and 365 nm light irradiation. The “petals” of the flower printed with NOCQDs display bright blue and green RTP after turning off 254 nm and 365 nm irradiation, respectively. Remarkably, distinct patterns emerge at various time intervals following the cessation of UV light, owing to the divergent RTP lifetimes displayed by the NCQDs coated on paper at 365 and 254 nm irradiation, as well as their disparities compared to the NOCQDs coated on paper. Specifically, within 1, 2, and 3 s after the UV light is turned off, three discernible patterns can be witnessed. For the purpose of this discussion, we assume that the pattern observed at the 2 s mark, subsequent to the discontinuation of UV light irradiation, under the stimulation of water and heating, confers valid information, so any observations made outside of this designated time window may result in the interpretation of incorrect information. It becomes crucial for users to determine an accurate time observation window by deciphering the cascading stimulus response characteristics of water and heating. Furthermore, employing a specific excitation wavelength becomes necessary to acquire a distinctive color for anti-counterfeiting patterns. Deviating from the appropriate time observation window would yield erroneous information, thereby markedly enhancing the security against counterfeit practices. It shows that the CQDs have the advantages of clear color and easy identification by human eyes and can be successfully applied to advanced anti-counterfeiting.

We also explore NCQDs and NOCQDs for time-dependent multi-stimuli responsive display of the encrypted information. In a 5D coding, the encrypted digit “3″ is not visible under UV irradiation due to the similar blue fluorescence color of NCQDs and NOCQDs (Fig. 10 ). Once the irradiation of 254 nm is switched off, the encrypted digit becomes visible, and the color and lifetime are different. The information “3” with cyan color can occur at 2 s. Once the irradiation of 365 nm is switched off, the information “3” with green color can arise at 3 s; the correct information “3” with designed color is concealed until reading out all five stimulus-, pattern-, time-, and color-dependent codes. Furthermore, a color/time-dependent encryption pattern responsive to external stimuli and excitation wavelengths is created using two types of RTP materials on a 2D plane, with different emission colors, positions (x, y), and time-dependent properties. Initially, these three colors are designated as three decimal numbers for encryption, with blue = 1, cyan = 2, green = 3, and the default dark (filter paper) = 0. To prepare the encrypted pattern, CQDs coated on paper with different colors is arranged according to specific rules to form “ ciphertext zone”, as shown in Fig. 10. We have encrypted the ciphertext to include uppercase and lowercase letters, making it difficult to decipher and suitable for practical applications. To decrypt, the encrypted matrix is illuminated under 254 and 365 nm light sources until all CQDs coated on paper are activated for RTP, and then the light sources are turned off to make the afterglow visible, and the dynamic afterglow processes of RTP are recorded. The color combinations of RTP within 2 s under excitation at 254 and 365 nm are read from the encrypted matrix, such as “2200” and “3311” in the third row, because of the excitation-dependent phosphorescence of the CQDs. The resulting decimal numbers are assembled and converted to binary in chronological order, and then recovered based on the ASCII code table. Furthermore, due to the quenching effect of oxygen in the air on RTP, the anti-counterfeiting patterns printed on NOCQDs and NCQDs will be completely deactivated after being left in the air for 30 min and can be repeatedly read after reheating. This self-hiding feature enables NOCQDs and NCQDs to provide higher anti-counterfeiting security and ensure information security. Therefore, this advanced cascaded stimuli-responsive multi-color time-dependent encryption technology can be easily implemented using NOCQDs and NCQDs, ensuring reliability and high security through pattern richness and unique time dependence.

In summary, we report the synthesis of CQDs with different surface functional groups, including –NH2, –COOH, and –OH, as well as NCQDs with N, O co-doping and N-doping. The relationship between the surface functional groups and the stimulus response characteristics and multicolor luminescence properties of the synthesized CQDs was investigated on a paper substrate. The hydrogen bonding between CQDs and paper was utilized to achieve water stimulus response. The active site of water stimulus response was found to be the hydrogen bond between the surface functional groups (–NH2, –COOH, and –OH) of NOCQDs. The hydrogen bonds between NOCQDs were shown to be quickly broken by water, promoting the formation of new hydrogen bonds between NOCQDs and paper. Removal of free water and oxygen by heating the paper was found to delay the infiltration of oxygen from the air into the interior of the paper, effectively protecting the phosphorescence from being quenched by oxygen. As a result, a multicolor RTP that responds to continuous water and heating stimuli was obtained. In addition, we found that the presence of a large number of functional groups that are prone to hydrogen bonding is crucial for achieving water stimulation response characteristics. The multicolor RTP emission of CQDs was found to be caused by the n-π* transition induced by the oxygen-containing functional groups (–COOH and –OH). Deprotonation of –COOH and –OH on the surface of NOCQDs can turn off their active site of water stimulation response. Furthermore, it was demonstrated that NOCQDs and NCQDs can be used for time-resolved dynamic anti-counterfeiting in response to multiple stimuli of water, heating, and excitation wavelengths. Overall, our study provides insights into the design and synthesis of CQDs with specific surface functional groups for achieving desirable stimulus response characteristics and multicolor luminescence properties.

L-histidine, 2,4,6-triaminopyrimidine, and thiazolium blue were purchased from Mayer and Aladdin Chem. Reagents Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used directly without further purification unless otherwise stated. Deionized water was produced through a Millipore water purification system (Milli-Q, Millipore) for the synthesis and for diluting samples for characterization.

Absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the samples were recorded by the Shimadzu UV-1750 spectrometer and a RF-6000 fluorescence spectrometer (Japan), respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 TEM at 200 kV to observe the morphologies of the NOCQDs and NCQDs. X-ray diffraction spectra (XRD) were determined on a Holland PANalytical X'Pert PRO X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα (λ = 1.54056 Å) as a radiation source in the 2θ range of 5-80˚. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a vertex70 spectrometer (KBr disk). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on Kratos AXIS UltraDLD (Kratos Analytical Ltd.) with mono Al Kα radiation (hν = 1487.71 eV) at a power of 75 W. The differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was studied using a NETZSCH DSC 200F3 with the heating rate of 2 °C/min and cooling rate of 2 °C/min. All phosphorescence emission spectra were measured a QuantaMaster 8000 spectrometer with the following configuration unless otherwise specified: lamp frequency, 50 Hz; initial delay, 500 ns; slit = 7 nm. The lifetimes of NOCQDs and NCQDs ethanol coated-paper were measured by time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) on a FLS980 spectrometer with excitation of 254 nm at room temperature. All fluorescence and phosphorescence images of the NOCQDs and NCQDs ethanol solution coated on paper upon excitation at a UV lamp (254 nm and 365 nm) were taken by a smartphone as the signal collector.

To synthesize NOCQDs and NCQDs, a simple one-step hydrothermal method was employed. L-histidine (0.2 mol) was dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water using ultrasonic treatment. The solution was then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon hydrothermal reactor, which was then placed in an air oven at 240 °C for 10 h. After the reaction was completed, the solution was allowed to cool down to room temperature. The as-prepared NOCQDs solution was subjected to centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min and thereafter dialyzed (MWCO: 1000 Da) to remove the deposition and unreacted substance. The purified NOCQDs powder was then obtained through freeze-drying. NCQDs were fabricated by using 2,4,6-triaminopyrimidine (0.2 mol) as raw materials. The preparation process was identical to that of NOCQDs. The resulting NOCQDs and NCQDs were dispersed in ethanol as a stock solution for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the NOCQDs and NCQDs inks
ce:section.ce:para: NOCQDs and NCQDs were dispersed in ethanol via ultra-sonication to form a uniform solution (NOCQDs: 6.0 mg/mL, NCQDs: 6.0 mg/mL). This solution was used as spraying printing ink and injected into a spray bottle. The predesigned patterns were printed onto a piece of commercial filter paper.

Dynamic anti-counterfeiting of NOCQDs and NCQDs: In this method, a 3D printed hollow model is employed to obstruct the filter paper. Subsequently, NCQDs and NOCQDs ethanol solution is loaded into a spray bottle. This solution is then uniformly sprayed onto the filter paper and paper money, allowing it to adhere exclusively to the exposed regions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cytotoxicity of NQCQDs and NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the cytotoxicity of NOCQDs and NCQDs, the standard MTT assay was performed. HL7702 cells were seeded in 96-well plates (NEST) at a density of 8 × 104 cells/mL. The cells were then exposed to different concentrations of NOCQDs and NCQDs aqueous solutions (0, 25, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 µg/mL) for 24 h. After incubation, the absorbance of each well was measured at 490 nm using a microplate reader, and the relative survival rate of the cells was calculated.

Li Yang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Qi Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yatuan Ma: Formal analysis, Investigation. Hongjuan Li: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Shiguo Sun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yongqian Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21978241), Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (2023-YBSF-224), Special Talent Project of Guangxi Science and Technology Base, China (Guike AD23026006) and the Scientific Research Project of Beibu Gulf University, China (2022KYQD17).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.151679.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
